reasoning: Both reviewers independently identify the same fundamental error at the heart of the manuscript: the paper treats cosmological redshift as a special-relativistic Doppler effect, assigns a global SR recession velocity to distant comoving sources, and then applies an additional SR aberration correction on top of the standard FLRW luminosity-distance formula. This double-counts effects already fully incorporated in the standard derivation via null geodesic propagation, scale-factor evolution, and the Etherington reciprocity theorem. Both reviewers rate this as retraction-worthy with very high confidence, and neither steelman exchange was triggered (L0), meaning neither reviewer found reason to downgrade their own severity. Multiple subsidiary findings—invalid Lorentz transformations across cosmological distances, circular preservation of the Etherington relation, and the invalid redshift-velocity mapping—all flow from this same core error and reinforce the conclusion. The paper's central quantitative result (an extra factor of (1+z) in d_L) and all downstream claims about revised cosmological parameters, dark energy reinterpretation, and CMB homogeneity are unsupported.

findings.finding: Core conceptual error: the paper treats cosmological redshift as a pure special-relativistic Doppler recession and adds a separate SR aberration correction to FLRW distance formulas, double-counting effects already encoded in the standard luminosity distance.
findings.justification: In FLRW cosmology, the luminosity distance d_L = (1+z) a_0 r already incorporates all relativistic effects: photon energy redshift, arrival-rate time dilation, and geometric area dilution. The manuscript's additional (1+z)^{-2} aberration factor on solid angle is not a new physical effect but a re-application of effects already present. Both reviewers identify this independently as the fatal flaw. If removed, the paper's central result d_L ∝ (1+z)^2 r collapses to the standard expression, and all downstream conclusions vanish.

findings.finding: Invalid use of a single Lorentz transformation between source and observer at cosmological separation, justified by the Equivalence Principle and a local 'auxiliary' frame.
findings.justification: The Equivalence Principle permits local inertial frames in the neighborhood of a single event, not a global SR frame linking distant events in curved spacetime. There is no unique, invariant relative velocity between distant comoving objects in FLRW. Applying the SR aberration formula tan(α'/2) = tan(α/2)·√((1−β)/(1+β)) across cosmological distances is physically meaningless. Both reviewers flag this as retraction-worthy. This error is inseparable from the core finding but constitutes an independently identifiable methodological violation.

findings.finding: The redshift-velocity mapping β = [(1+z)²−1]/[(1+z)²+1] via the SR Doppler formula is invalid as a basis for computing aberration in FLRW cosmology.
findings.justification: Cosmological redshift in FLRW spacetime arises from cumulative expansion along the photon's path, not from a single inertial-frame Doppler shift. Multiple inequivalent velocity definitions exist (Fermi, areal, affine-parameter, etc.), and none reduces to the simple SR inversion used here in a way that would license subsequent SR aberration calculations. Since the entire aberration correction is built on β(z), the invalidity of this mapping propagates to all quantitative results. Both reviewers identify this.

findings.finding: The claim that the Etherington reciprocity relation is 'preserved' under the new definitions is circular/tautological.
findings.justification: The Etherington relation d_L = (1+z)² d_A is a theorem that holds under broad conditions in any metric theory with photon number conservation. The paper redefines both d_L and d_A simultaneously (inserting extra factors of (1+z) in each) so that they trivially satisfy the relation. This is not a derivation of a new physical law; it is an internal consistency check on a redefinition. The paper offers no independent observational validation that the new d_L(z) matches data. Both reviewers flag this, with Review A rating it MAJOR-REVISION and Review B rating it RETRACTION-WORTHY. Given that this circular reasoning is what the paper uses to claim its framework is self-consistent, and that the underlying redefinitions are themselves invalid (per the core error), this rises to retraction-worthy.

findings.finding: Equation (16): d_L = R³(t₀)/R²(t₁) · r₁ is inconsistent with the standard FLRW result and follows from the flawed aberration argument.
findings.justification: The standard result is d_L = a₀ r₁ (1+z) = (a₀²/a₁) r₁. The manuscript's expression includes an extra factor of (1+z) arising entirely from the invalid aberration correction. This is the paper's principal quantitative claim, and it is wrong. Identified by Review A; consistent with Review B's identification of the same issue in the context of equations (11)-(16).

findings.finding: Equation (29): d_A := r_O(1+z) contradicts the standard angular-diameter distance and stems from the same invalid SR aberration reasoning.
findings.justification: The standard angular-diameter distance is d_A = a(t₁) r₁ = r₁/(1+z) in comoving coordinates. The paper instead gets d_A = r_O(1+z) by applying its aberration argument to angular sizes. This altered d_A is central to the paper's preservation of the Etherington relation and its cosmological conclusions. Identified by Review A.

findings.finding: The statement that objects with superluminal recession velocity are invisible is false.
findings.justification: In standard FLRW cosmology, objects currently receding faster than c can still be observed if their light was emitted when they were within the past light cone. This is well-established (Davis & Lineweaver 2004). The error reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of cosmological horizons and undermines confidence in the velocity-based framework, but alone would not require retraction. However, it is used to set the domain of validity of the paper's key equations.

findings.finding: The qualitative CMB claims (aberration 'spreads' and homogenizes the CMB) are unsupported and contradict standard treatments.
findings.justification: Standard analyses of aberration effects on the CMB (from observer peculiar motion) show dipole modulation and specific mode-coupling patterns, not a generic smoothing that would explain isotropy. The paper provides no quantitative derivation and the qualitative argument is physically incorrect. Both reviewers flag this. While the claim is striking, the section is explicitly labeled qualitative and does not constitute the paper's central result, so MAJOR-REVISION is appropriate.

findings.finding: Equations (34)/(36) for H(z) appear malformed or algebraically inconsistent with standard FLRW Friedmann equations.
findings.justification: The standard dimensionless Hubble parameter is E(z)² = Ω_M(1+z)³ + Ω_k(1+z)² + Ω_Λ. The manuscript's expressions do not match this form. If the distance-redshift integrals use incorrect H(z), all quantitative cosmological parameter estimates are unreliable. However, this may partly be a notational issue and is secondary to the core conceptual error. Identified by Review A.

findings.finding: Curvature normalization in equations (37)/(39) is handled inconsistently with the standard S_k(χ)/√|Ω_k| structure.
findings.justification: The mixing of r₁, F, and √|Ω_k| prefactors is not transparently correct. Since the paper argues curvature constraints change, errors here would affect quantitative predictions. Identified by Review A.

findings.finding: The use of Eq. (3), v_i = d(g_{ik} x^k)/dτ, as justification for an observable recession velocity relevant to aberration is not valid.
findings.justification: Differentiating covariant position components with respect to proper time does not yield a physically meaningful or unique recession velocity relevant to observed aberration in cosmology. This is used to motivate the entire SR-like treatment. Identified by Review A.

findings.finding: No quantitative comparison to observational data (SNe Ia, CMB power spectra) is provided despite strong claims about cosmological parameter shifts.
findings.justification: Even if the theoretical framework were correct, the absence of data fitting means the claimed parameter revisions are purely speculative. However, this is secondary to the fundamental theoretical errors. Identified by Review B.

findings.finding: Extension of the flawed aberration logic to gravitational waves is speculative and unsupported.
findings.justification: Presented as an aside; does not affect the paper's main validity assessment. Identified by Review A.

findings.finding: Presentation problems: duplicated paragraphs, truncated sentences, missing displayed equations, unclear notation.
findings.justification: These impede review and reproducibility but are secondary to the deeper methodological flaws. Identified by Review A.

review_a.reasoning: The manuscript’s central claim—that standard cosmological distance measures are missing an extra relativistic-aberration dimming factor, leading to d_L being larger by an additional factor of (1+z)—is methodologically unsound. In FLRW cosmology, the standard luminosity-distance and angular-diameter-distance relations already incorporate the relevant kinematic and geometric effects for comoving sources and null geodesic propagation. The paper incorrectly treats cosmological redshift as an ordinary special-relativistic Doppler recession between distant source and observer, then applies a separate SR aberration correction on top of the standard FLRW derivation, which double-counts effects already encoded in the spacetime geometry and reciprocity relations. Several equations are also plainly incorrect or internally inconsistent (e.g., superluminal recession claim implying invisibility; malformed H(z); inconsistent curvature normalization; invalid use of Lorentz transformations between distant cosmological frames). If these issues are confirmed, the main conclusions about revised cosmological parameters, altered supernova distances, and qualitative CMB reinterpretation are not supported.

findings.finding: Core conceptual error: the paper treats cosmological redshift as a pure special-relativistic Doppler recession and then adds a separate SR aberration correction to FLRW distance formulas.
findings.justification: This is the central methodological flaw. In FLRW cosmology, redshift, flux dilution, and angular-size relations are derived from null geodesics, expansion, and Etherington reciprocity. Recasting redshift as ordinary relative velocity between distant comoving frames and then applying SR aberration as an extra effect double-counts the kinematic/geometric content already present. If this is wrong, the main result d_L ∝ (1+z)^2 r instead of the standard d_L ∝ (1+z) a_0 r collapses.

findings.finding: Invalid use of a single Lorentz transformation between source and observer at cosmological separation, justified by the Equivalence Principle and a nearby 'auxiliary' frame.
findings.justification: The Equivalence Principle permits local inertial frames, not a global SR treatment between distant events in curved spacetime. The manuscript explicitly acknowledges quasi-translations are not Lorentz transformations, yet proceeds as if one can define a meaningful global relative velocity and apply Einstein 1905 aberration formulas across cosmological distances. This invalidates the derivation of the extra angular and flux factors.

findings.finding: The derivation of the extra factor dΩ' = dΩ/(1+z)^2 and the resulting flux suppression is a double-counting of effects already contained in standard luminosity-distance derivations.
findings.justification: Standard cosmology already gives F = L / (4π d_L^2) with d_L = (1+z)^2 d_A and includes one factor from photon energy redshift, one from arrival-rate time dilation, and the geometric area relation from propagation in FLRW. The paper inserts an additional beaming/aberration factor as though the source were moving through a local inertial frame relative to the observer. If removed, the claimed revised d_L and all downstream cosmological reinterpretations disappear.

findings.finding: Equation (4)/(5) is used as a general mapping from cosmological redshift z to source speed β, implying a unique recession velocity from the SR Doppler formula.
findings.justification: In cosmology there is no unique invariant global relative velocity between distant comoving objects; many velocity definitions exist, and the simple SR Doppler inversion is not generally valid for FLRW redshift over large separations. The paper builds its entire aberration correction on β(z). If this mapping is invalid, the correction cannot be computed as presented.

findings.finding: The statement 'Equation 5 clearly doesn't hold if the expansion of the Universe between us and the CO is faster than c, but in that case we simply can't detect any light signal from the CO' is false.
findings.justification: Objects with superluminal recession in standard cosmology can still be observable, depending on the expansion history and horizon structure. This indicates serious misunderstanding of cosmological kinematics. While this single statement alone would not force retraction, it strongly undermines confidence in the paper's velocity-based framework.

findings.finding: Equation (16) for luminosity distance, d_L = R^3(t0)/R^2(t1) r1, is inconsistent with standard FLRW results and is obtained from the flawed flux argument.
findings.justification: The standard result is d_L = (a_0^2/a_1) r1 = a_0 r1 (1+z), not a_0 r1 (1+z)^2. The manuscript's central quantitative claim is exactly this extra factor. Since it arises from an invalid derivation, the principal conclusion that distances and inferred cosmological parameters have been systematically mismeasured is unsupported.

findings.finding: The manuscript claims the Etherington relation is 'restored' after redefining both d_L and d_A, but this is achieved by altering both definitions in tandem rather than deriving a physically new reciprocity law.
findings.justification: Etherington reciprocity is a theorem under broad assumptions (metric theory, null geodesics, photon number conservation). If one modifies d_L by hand and then adjusts d_A to preserve reciprocity, that does not validate the new physics. This weakens the manuscript's internal consistency argument, though the deeper problem is already fatal.

findings.finding: Equation (29), d_A := r_O (1+z), contradicts the standard angular-diameter distance scaling and stems from a nonstandard 'assistant frame' argument that is not rigorously derived.
findings.justification: The angular-diameter distance in FLRW decreases relative to proper distance by factors of scale expansion; the manuscript instead inserts an extra (1+z) based on SR-style aberration reasoning. This is central to preserving its altered Etherington relation and therefore to its cosmological conclusions.

findings.finding: The paper conflates local aberration effects from peculiar velocity with cosmological propagation effects for comoving sources.
findings.justification: Relativistic aberration is certainly relevant for an observer/source with local peculiar motion relative to the cosmic rest frame, but that is not the same as assigning every comoving source an SR recession velocity and applying beaming formulas. This confusion affects the interpretation of supernova fluxes and the qualitative CMB discussion.

findings.finding: Equations (34) and (36) defining the redshift dependence of the expansion history are malformed/inconsistent with standard FLRW expressions.
findings.justification: The standard dimensionless expansion function is E(z)^2 = Ω_M(1+z)^3 + Ω_k(1+z)^2 + Ω_Λ. The manuscript instead gives unusual expressions such as H(z',Ω_M,Ω_Λ) := (1 + z'^2)(1 + Ω_M z') - z'Ω_Λ(2 + z'), which appears algebraically incorrect or at least nonstandard and dimensionally suspect. If the distance-redshift integrals are based on this incorrect form, quantitative conclusions are unreliable.

findings.finding: Equation (37)/(39) mishandles curvature normalization by using F(√|Ω_k| ∫...) without clearly preserving the standard S_k(χ)/√|Ω_k| structure and with inconsistent notation for r1.
findings.justification: Curvature-dependent distance formulas require careful normalization. The manuscript's notation mixes r1, F, and √|Ω_k| prefactors in a way that is not transparently correct. Since the paper explicitly argues curvature constraints may change, any inconsistency here matters materially.

findings.finding: The use of Eq. (3), v_i = d(g_ik x^k)/dτ, as justification that metric evolution itself produces an observed relative velocity is not a valid derivation of recession velocity relevant to observed aberration.
findings.justification: Differentiating covariant position components is not a standard or meaningful route to observable recession kinematics in cosmology. This argument is used to motivate the entire SR-like treatment; if corrected, the conceptual foundation weakens substantially.

findings.finding: The manuscript repeatedly asserts that because Lorentz contraction and time dilation are 'contained in FLRW,' relativistic aberration must therefore generate an additional correction to cosmological fluxes.
findings.justification: This inference is not valid. The presence of local SR behavior inside a GR spacetime does not imply one may append separate SR aberration factors to global cosmological observables. This is a substantive conceptual overreach that could change conclusions.

findings.finding: The qualitative CMB claims are unsupported and likely opposite to established treatment of aberration from observer motion relative to the CMB.
findings.justification: The manuscript argues cosmological aberration would 'mix' and smooth anisotropies, potentially undermining isotropy itself, but provides no correct derivation. Established CMB aberration analyses concern local peculiar motion and induce specific mode coupling, not the claimed global smoothing from expansion. These claims could materially mislead readers even if labeled qualitative.

findings.finding: The manuscript extends the same flawed flux-aberration logic to gravitational waves without a derivation.
findings.justification: This is speculative and unsupported, but presented as an aside. It does not by itself determine the paper's main validity.

findings.finding: Substantial reporting/presentation problems: duplicated paragraphs, truncated sentences, missing displayed equations in the prose, and unclear notation transitions.
findings.justification: These issues impede review and reproducibility but are secondary to the deeper methodological flaws.

review_b.reasoning: The manuscript contains fundamental methodological errors that invalidate its central claims. The core argument — that relativistic aberration alters the effective solid angle for flux measurements in an FLRW universe by a factor (1+z)^{-2} beyond standard effects — misapplies special relativistic aberration formulas to a curved, expanding spacetime where the standard luminosity distance already correctly accounts for all relativistic beaming effects. The derivation violates the basic principles of general relativistic photon propagation and the standard etherington relation, which the author claims to preserve only by redefining distances in a circular manner. Additionally, there are internal mathematical inconsistencies (e.g., contradictory statements about angular size changes) and a misunderstanding of how the cosmological principle and FLRW metric already incorporate the effects of recession. The conclusions — that dark energy might be overestimated, the CMB homogeneity could be an artifact, and cosmological parameters need revision — are unsupported by the flawed analysis.

findings.finding: The central claim that relativistic aberration introduces an additional (1+z)^{-2} flux reduction in an expanding FLRW universe is based on a misapplication of special relativistic aberration formulas.
findings.justification: The standard luminosity distance formula in FLRW cosmology already fully incorporates all relativistic effects (time dilation, energy redshift, and geometric effects) via the factor (1+z)^2 in the relation d_L = (1+z)^2 d_A. The author's attempt to add another (1+z)^{-2} from 'aberration' is double-counting. The derivation in equations (11)-(16) incorrectly treats the telescope's angular size in the comoving frame as if it transforms via special relativistic aberration from the source's rest frame, ignoring that the FLRW metric already defines the correct angular diameter distance. If this error were published, it would lead to a fundamentally incorrect expression for luminosity distance, undermining all subsequent claims about cosmological parameters.

findings.finding: The derivation of the aberration effect (equations 9-12) incorrectly applies flat-space Minkowski aberration to photons traveling on curved null geodesics in an expanding universe.
findings.justification: The author uses the special relativistic aberration formula (tan α transformation) between a local 'auxiliary' frame and the observer's frame, then claims this affects the solid angle of a distant telescope as seen from the source. However, in FLRW spacetime, null geodesics are not straight lines in Minkowski space; the angular diameter distance already accounts for the mapping between angles at emission and reception. The paper's Fig. 2.1 and associated reasoning ignore that the comoving observer's telescope is not in a local inertial frame with constant relative velocity β to the source; the recession velocity is a coordinate effect, not a proper relative velocity that allows simple Lorentz transformations between distant events.

findings.finding: The paper claims that the Etherington relation (d_L = (1+z)^2 d_A) survives their correction, but this is only achieved by redefining distances in a circular manner.
findings.justification: The author derives d_L = (1+z)^2 r_O in equation (24) and d_A = r_O (1+z) in equation (29), which trivially satisfies the Etherington relation. However, the standard derivation links r_O to the comoving distance via the FLRW metric, while the author's 'aberration' correction changes how r_O relates to the redshift integral. The claim that the relation 'still holds' is tautological because both d_L and d_A have been redefined consistently; the physical question is whether the new d_L matches observations. The paper provides no demonstration that the new d_L(z) fits supernova data; instead, it speculates on parameter shifts without quantitative comparison.

findings.finding: The treatment of CMB anisotropies (Section 3.3) is physically inconsistent: the author claims aberration 'spreads' the CMB and increases isotropy, contradicting standard results that relativistic aberration generates a dipole and boosts power at high l.
findings.justification: The qualitative description of CMB aberration is internally contradictory. The paper states that the small-angle approximation gives a factor 10^6 increase in solid angle, 'mixing' anisotropies. However, standard kinetic theory in cosmology (e.g., Chluba & Sunyaev 2004) shows that our motion relative to the CMB rest frame produces a dipole and quadrupole coupling, but does not 'spread' the entire CMB to make it more isotropic. The claim that 'the CMB homogeneity may be the result of this mixing' is unsupported speculation that, if taken seriously, would require a retraction of standard CMB interpretation, but the argument here is too vague and mathematically incomplete to be assessed as retraction-worthy itself.

findings.finding: The derivation of the redshift-velocity relation (equation 5) uses the special relativistic Doppler formula for recession, but in FLRW cosmology, redshift is not purely a Doppler effect; it is a cumulative expansion effect.
findings.justification: The paper assumes that the observed cosmological redshift can be interpreted as a relative velocity via β = [(1+z)^2-1]/[(1+z)^2+1], treating the expansion as analogous to inertial motion. This is a known misinterpretation: in FLRW, redshift is due to the stretching of space, not Doppler shift from a relative velocity that obeys special relativity. The paper's subsequent application of Lorentz transformations between the source and observer at different cosmic epochs is therefore invalid. This is a foundational error that affects all subsequent calculations.

findings.finding: The paper does not provide any quantitative comparison to data (e.g., SNe Ia or CMB power spectra), despite making strong claims about cosmological parameter shifts.
findings.justification: While the methodological errors are fatal, the lack of data comparison is a minor concern in the context of this review. Even if the theory were correct, the paper would need to fit observed distance moduli to claim that Ω_M and Ω_Λ change. The paper only speculates qualitatively. This does not directly invalidate the central error but highlights incompleteness.

judge.severity_agreement: Both reviews agree that the core conceptual error (treating cosmological redshift as SR Doppler and adding an extra aberration factor) is retraction-worthy. They also agree that the related finding regarding the invalid use of Lorentz transformations between distant frames is retraction-worthy.
shared_findings: Core conceptual error: manuscript treats cosmological redshift as SR Doppler and adds separate aberration correction, double-counting effects in FLRW.
Invalid use of Lorentz transformations between source and observer at cosmological separation (rejection of global SR treatment in curved spacetime).
Derivation of extra flux/aberration factor double-counts effects already in standard d_L derivation.
Claim that Etherington relation is 'restored' is circular/tautological (redefining distances to preserve the relation).
Redshift-velocity mapping via SR Doppler formula is invalid in FLRW cosmology.
Qualitative CMB claims are unsupported and contradict standard treatment (mixing/homogeneity claim).
severity_disputes: Finding regarding the statement that objects with superluminal recession are invisible: Review A rates this as MAJOR-REVISION, Review B does not mention this specific statement.

Finding regarding conflation of local aberration with cosmological propagation: Review A rates as MAJOR-REVISION; Review B does not separately list this but subsumes it under the retraction-worthy core error.
Finding regarding malformed/inconsistent H(z) equations: Review A rates as MAJOR-REVISION; Review B does not list this explicitly.
Finding regarding curvature normalization issues: Review A rates as MAJOR-REVISION; Review B does not list this explicitly.
Finding regarding invalid use of Eq. (3) for recession velocity: Review A rates as MAJOR-REVISION; Review B does not mention this.

Finding regarding the manuscript repeatedly asserting that because SR effects are contained in FLRW, aberration must be added: Review A rates as MAJOR-REVISION; Review B does not explicitly list this.
unique_to_a: Statement about invisible superluminal sources is false.
Invalid use of Eq. (3) to derive recession velocity.
Argument that because Lorentz contraction/time dilation are in FLRW, aberration must be an extra correction.
Extension of flaw to gravitational waves (speculative aside).
unique_to_b: Paper does not provide quantitative comparison to data (e.g., SNe Ia or CMB power spectra).

This double-counts effects already fully incorporated in the standard derivation via null geodesic propagation, scale-factor evolution, and the Etherington reciprocity theorem. Both reviewers rate this as retraction-worthy with very high confidence, and neither steelman exchange was triggered (L0), meaning neither reviewer found reason to downgrade their own severity. Multiple subsidiary findings—invalid Lorentz transformations across cosmological distances, circular preservation of the Etherington relation, and the invalid redshift-velocity mapping—all flow from this same core error and reinforce the conclusion. The paper's central quantitative result (an extra factor of (1+z) in d_L) and all downstream claims about revised cosmological parameters, dark energy reinterpretation, and CMB homogeneity are unsupported.
